It refers to the future cost incurred when quick or expedient solutions are chosen over sound ones — through shortcuts, missing tests or poor design. Like financial debt, it accrues interest: as debt accumulates, every change becomes slower and riskier. A healthy process makes technical debt visible, decides deliberately when to take it on, and allocates time to repay it, rather than letting it grow unchecked until progress stalls.

Maturity models offer a framework for assessing and evolving a process over time. Models such as the Capability Maturity Model Integration (CMMI) describe stages from ad-hoc and unpredictable work up to managed, defined, measured and continuously optimizing processes. Such models help an organization understand its current level and identify concrete steps to advance, though they must be applied pragmatically so that process discipline serves the delivery of value rather than becoming an end in itself.

Improvement must always be tied back to requirements and to the delivery of value. A faster or more disciplined process is only worthwhile if it produces software that meets its requirements and serves its users better. Therefore process evaluation should include whether the software actually satisfies functional and non-functional requirements, whether it is delivered on time, and whether users are satisfied, keeping the focus on outcomes rather than on activity for its own sake.

Continuous improvement is best pursued as a cycle: plan a change, implement it, measure the effect and act on the result, then repeat — the Plan-Do-Check-Act loop. Improvements are introduced as small, measurable experiments, kept if they help and reversed if they do not. This experimental, evidence-driven attitude, applied steadily over time, is what distinguishes teams that get better from those that merely stay busy, and it turns process improvement into a permanent habit.

Thus, evaluating and improving the code-development process means choosing an appropriate methodology, measuring both product and process with well-chosen metrics used wisely, applying practices such as code review and retrospectives, managing technical debt and shortening feedback loops, all directed at delivering software that meets its requirements; treated as a continuous, evidence-based cycle, this discipline steadily raises a team's quality, speed and reliability.
questions: Why is it necessary to evaluate and improve the development process and not only the finished product?
How do Waterfall and Agile methodologies differ in the way they handle changing requirements?
What is the difference between product metrics and process metrics, and give an example of each?
What does Goodhart's law warn about when using metrics, and how should metrics therefore be used?
What is technical debt, and how does a healthy development process manage it?

title: Developing and documenting an API according to requirements
objective: To demonstrate, through the design, implementation and documentation of a working web API, the integrated ability to translate requirements into a correct, well-structured and clearly documented programming interface.
theory: An API (Application Programming Interface) is a defined contract through which software components communicate: it specifies the operations available, the data they accept and return, and the rules for using them, while hiding the internal implementation. A well-designed web API lets different applications and teams integrate reliably, and its quality is judged by how faithfully it meets its requirements, how consistent and predictable it is, and how well it is documented so that others can use it without reading its source code.

The dominant style for web APIs is REST, which models the system as resources identified by URLs and manipulated through standard HTTP methods: GET to retrieve, POST to create, PUT or PATCH to update and DELETE to remove. REST APIs use HTTP status codes to signal outcomes — 200 for success, 201 for creation, 400 for a bad request, 401 for unauthorized, 404 for not found and 500 for server errors — and typically exchange data as JSON. Consistent, correct use of methods and status codes is a hallmark of a professional API.

Good API design follows established principles: resources are named clearly and consistently, usually with plural nouns; input is validated and meaningful error messages are returned; the interface is versioned so that changes do not break existing clients; and access is secured through authentication and authorization. Documentation is an integral part of the deliverable, and the OpenAPI (Swagger) specification is the industry standard for describing an API in a machine-readable form that can also generate human-readable, interactive documentation.
materials: A computer with a programming environment and a web framework suitable for building APIs (for example, Python with Flask or FastAPI, Node.js with Express, or an equivalent).
A tool for testing HTTP requests, such as curl, Postman or an interactive Swagger UI.
A data store appropriate to the task (an in-memory structure, a file, or a small database).
The OpenAPI/Swagger tooling for writing and rendering the API specification.
A written set of requirements for the API to be built and a report template for the documentation.
procedure: Analyse the given requirements and identify the resources, the operations on each resource, the data fields, and the validation and security rules the API must satisfy.
Design the API contract: define the endpoints with their HTTP methods and URLs, the request and response formats in JSON, and the status codes for success and error cases.
Implement the endpoints in the chosen framework, connecting them to the data store, and include input validation and correct status-code responses for both valid and invalid requests.
Add error handling and at least a basic security measure such as an API key or token, and ensure errors return clear, structured messages rather than exposing internal details.

Write the API documentation using the OpenAPI specification, describing every endpoint, its parameters, its request and response bodies and its possible responses, and render it as interactive documentation.
Test the complete API against the requirements using the HTTP tool, verifying correct behaviour for normal, edge and error cases, and record the results in the report.
tasks: Design and implement a RESTful web API for a defined resource (for example, a catalogue of items or a task list) that supports the full set of create, read, update and delete operations, using correct HTTP methods and status codes.
Add input validation, structured error responses and a basic authentication mechanism to the API, and demonstrate that invalid or unauthorized requests are rejected with appropriate status codes.
Produce complete OpenAPI documentation for the API and demonstrate, through the interactive documentation or a request tool, that every documented endpoint behaves exactly as described.
conclusion: As a result of the work, the student has demonstrated the integrated ability to translate requirements into a correct, secure and well-structured RESTful API and to document it clearly and accurately using the OpenAPI standard.
questions: What is an API, and what does it mean to describe it as a contract between software components?
How are HTTP methods and status codes used to express operations and their outcomes in a REST API?
Why is input validation and structured error handling important in an API, and what security measures protect it?
Why should an API be versioned, and how does versioning protect existing clients?
What is the purpose of the OpenAPI (Swagger) specification, and why is documentation considered part of API development?
